schools.summary: Abram returns from Egypt: 'and Abram went up out of Egypt, he, and his wife, and all that he had, and Lot with him, into the south' — Abram returning from Egypt (going 'up' toward the higher land of Canaan) with his wife Sarai (restored, 12:19), his nephew Lot, and all his possessions (the patriarch, delivered from Egypt, coming back to the land of promise, the return from the place of his fault and trial); Abram coming up with Sarai (restored to him), Lot, and all his substance (increased in Egypt, 12:16, the household of the patriarch returning intact, God's mercy bringing him safely out, with his wife and goods, despite his fault); 'into the south' (Abram coming into the south, the Negev, the southern part of Canaan, where he had been before going to Egypt, 12:9, the patriarch retracing his steps, returning to the land of promise and to his worship, vv.3-4, the pilgrim back in Canaan, to renew his sojourn) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Abram went up out of Egypt, he, and his wife, and all that he had, and Lot with him, into the south
Abram's going up out of Egypt back to Canaan
the patriarch, delivered and restored, returning to the land of promise
Sarai restored and the substance increased (12:16, 19)

schools.summary: Abram's riches: 'and Abram was very rich in cattle, in silver, and in gold' — Abram having become very wealthy, abounding in livestock (cattle, the chief wealth of the pastoral patriarch), silver, and gold (his riches, increased in Egypt, 12:16, the great substance of the patriarch, a mark of God's blessing on him); Abram's wealth chiefly in cattle (flocks and herds, the substance of a nomadic herdsman, his great flocks and herds, the wealth that would soon require the parting from Lot, vv.6-7, the land not bearing them both); 'in silver, and in gold' (Abram also having silver and gold, the patriarch rich in precious metals as well as livestock, the blessing of God on Abram in temporal things, yet his true riches the promises of God, and he remaining a pilgrim and stranger, Hebrews 11:9, not setting his heart on his wealth) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Abram was very rich in cattle, in silver, and in gold
Abram's great riches
the great substance of the pilgrim patriarch, a mark of God's blessing
the wealth that would require the parting from Lot (vv.6-7)

schools.summary: Abram returns to Bethel: 'and he went on his journeys from the south even to Bethel, unto the place where his tent had been at the beginning, between Bethel and Hai' — Abram traveling by stages from the Negev back to Bethel, to the very place where he had pitched his tent and built an altar before going to Egypt (12:8, the patriarch returning to the place of his former worship, retracing his steps to where he had called on the LORD); Abram returning to 'the place where his tent had been at the beginning' (to the spot of his earlier altar and worship, 12:8, the patriarch, after the trial and fault of Egypt, returning to the place and the practice of his devotion, the renewal of his worship after his lapse, the believer returning to his first works); 'between Bethel and Hai (Ai)' (the place of Abram's former encampment and altar, 12:8, the patriarch coming back to where he had worshiped, to renew his calling on the name of the LORD, v.4, the return to the altar marking the restoration of his communion with God after Egypt) — Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: And he went on his journeys from the south even to Bethel, unto the place where his tent had been at the beginning, between Bethel and Hai
Abram's return to Bethel, the place of his former altar
the patriarch returning to the place and practice of his devotion after Egypt
the believer returning to his first works

schools.summary: Abram's renewed worship: 'unto the place of the altar, which he had made there at the first: and there Abram called on the name of the LORD' — Abram coming to the place of the altar he had built at the first (12:8), and there renewing his worship, calling on the name of the LORD (the patriarch resuming his public worship of God at his former altar, the restoration of his devotion after Egypt); Abram returning to 'the place of the altar... at the first' (to the altar of his earlier worship, 12:8, the patriarch, after his lapse and deliverance, coming back to the altar, the believer renewing his worship at the place of his former communion); 'there Abram called on the name of the LORD' (Abram again worshiping and praying, calling on the LORD, as at the first, 12:8, the public profession and worship renewed, the patriarch, humbled by his fault in Egypt, returning to call on God, the restoration of communion and worship, the believer's return to prayer and praise) — Gill.

themes: Unto the place of the altar, which he had made there at the first: and there Abram called on the name of the LORD
Abram's renewed worship at the altar
the patriarch renewing his devotion at the place of his former communion
the believer's return to prayer and praise after his lapse

schools.summary: Lot's wealth: 'and Lot also, which went with Abram, had flocks, and herds, and tents' — Lot, Abram's nephew, who had accompanied him, also having become wealthy, possessing his own flocks, herds, and tents (the prosperity of Lot, sharing, perhaps under God's blessing on Abram, in the increase, the substance of Lot that, with Abram's, would prove too great for the land, v.6); Lot having flocks, herds, and tents (the wealth of a pastoral household, Lot prospering alongside Abram, the increase of both men's substance setting the stage for the strife, v.7, and the parting, vv.8-9); Lot's prosperity, with Abram's (v.2), making their joint dwelling impossible (v.6, the very blessing of increase becoming the occasion of strife between their herdsmen, the wealth that should have been a benefit bringing a difficulty, the setting for Abram's generous parting from Lot, vv.8-9) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Lot also, which went with Abram, had flocks, and herds, and tents
Lot's own wealth in flocks, herds, and tents
the nephew prospering alongside Abram
the wealth that would prove too great for the land and bring the strife

schools.summary: The land cannot bear them: 'and the land was not able to bear them, that they might dwell together: for their substance was great, so that they could not dwell together' — the land (the pasturage and water available) unable to support both Abram's and Lot's great flocks and herds together (their combined substance too great for the region, especially as the Canaanite and Perizzite also held the land, v.7, the abundance of both making their joint dwelling impossible); the land unable to bear them (the pasturage insufficient for both households' livestock, the very increase that God had blessed them with now straining the land, the difficulty of two great pastoral households dwelling in the same limited region); 'their substance was great, so that they could not dwell together' (the greatness of their wealth forcing a separation, the blessing of abundance becoming the occasion of difficulty and, through the herdsmen, of strife, v.7, the need for Abram and Lot to part, the setting for Abram's generous proposal, vv.8-9) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the land was not able to bear them, that they might dwell together: for their substance was great, so that they could not dwell together
the land could not bear Abram and Lot together
the abundance of both households too great for the region
the blessing of wealth becoming the occasion of difficulty and parting

schools.summary: The strife of the herdsmen: 'and there was a strife between the herdmen of Abram's cattle and the herdmen of Lot's cattle: and the Canaanite and the Perizzite dwelled then in the land' — the servants of Abram and Lot quarreling (over pasturage and water, the land being too strait, v.6, the strife between the herdsmen threatening the peace between the households, the occasion of the parting, the contention that Abram would resolve by generous separation, vv.8-9); the strife arising among the herdsmen (over the limited pasture, the contention, if unchecked, breeding division and reproach, the quarrel of the servants endangering the unity of the family, Abram's wisdom and charity to settle it, vv.8-9); 'the Canaanite and the Perizzite dwelled then in the land' (the note marking that the heathen inhabitants, Canaanites and Perizzites, were present, watching, the strife between the servants of the godly a scandal before the heathen, dishonoring God and the true religion, the presence of the watching pagans making the contention the more grievous, and Abram's peacemaking the more necessary, lest the name of God be reproached) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And there was a strife between the herdmen of Abram's cattle and the herdmen of Lot's cattle: and the Canaanite and the Perizzite dwelled then in the land
the strife between the herdsmen and the presence of the Canaanite and Perizzite
the quarrel over pasturage threatening the family's peace
the watching heathen making the strife a scandal, calling for peacemaking

schools.summary: Abram's appeal for peace: 'and Abram said unto Lot, Let there be no strife, I pray thee, between me and thee, and between my herdmen and thy herdmen; for we be brethren' — Abram, the elder and the greater, taking the initiative to end the strife (he seeking peace, deprecating contention between himself and Lot and between their servants, the patriarch's charity and humility, seeking peace rather than standing on his rights, the peacemaker, Matthew 5:9); Abram seeking to remove all strife — between the principals and between the servants (the patriarch, concerned for peace and for the honor of God before the heathen, v.7, not letting the quarrel continue, his wisdom in addressing the root of the contention); 'for we be brethren' (Abram appealing to their kinship, uncle and nephew, near kin, brethren ought not to strive, the bond of family and of faith holding them in peace, the appeal to brotherhood as the ground of concord, Abram's charity, valuing peace and kinship above pasturage and rights) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Henry.

themes: And Abram said unto Lot, Let there be no strife, I pray thee, between me and thee, and between my herdmen and thy herdmen; for we be brethren
Abram's gracious appeal for peace with Lot
the patriarch seeking peace and appealing to kinship, the peacemaker (Matthew 5:9)
valuing concord above pasturage and his own rights

schools.summary: Abram offers Lot first choice: 'is not the whole land before thee? separate thyself, I pray thee, from me: if thou wilt take the left hand, then I will go to the right; or if thou depart to the right hand, then I will go to the left' — Abram proposing a peaceful separation (the whole land lying open before them, with room enough, Abram urging Lot to part from him, that they might dwell apart and the strife cease, the wise and generous proposal to end the contention by separation); Abram, the elder and the greater (to whom the land was promised), giving Lot the first choice — 'if thou wilt take the left hand, then I will go to the right' (Abram waiving his right of precedence, yielding the choice to his younger kinsman, the remarkable charity and humility of Abram, preferring peace and his nephew's good to his own advantage); Abram's offer of first choice showing his faith and self-denial (he trusting God for his portion and not grasping at the best for himself, the man to whom the whole land was promised letting his nephew choose first, the generosity that springs from faith, Abram content to take what was left, trusting God's promise, the pattern of charity and peaceableness) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: Is not the whole land before thee? separate thyself, I pray thee, from me: if thou wilt take the left hand, then I will go to the right
Abram's generous offer of first choice to Lot
the patriarch waiving his precedence, giving his nephew first choice
the self-denial and faith of Abram, content to take what was left

schools.summary: Lot's worldly choice: 'and Lot lifted up his eyes, and beheld all the plain of Jordan, that it was well watered every where, before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah, even as the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt, as thou comest unto Zoar' — Lot, given the choice (v.9), looking and seeing the plain of Jordan, well watered and fertile (he choosing by the sight of his eyes, by what seemed best for his flocks and his prosperity, the choice governed by worldly advantage, not by spiritual considerations); the plain, at that time (before God destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah, ch. 19), as fair as 'the garden of the LORD (Eden)' or 'the land of Egypt' (the well-watered Nile valley, the land looking like paradise, but its fairness deceptive, it lying under the shadow of coming judgment, and near the wicked cities, v.13, the narrator's note, 'before the LORD destroyed Sodom,' marking the irony of Lot's choice); Lot choosing by the eye, looking to the richness of the land and his own gain, taking no account of the wickedness of Sodom (v.13) or of the danger to his soul (the choice of the worldly man, walking by sight, the fair appearance masking the spiritual peril, the contrast with Abram, who walked by faith, Lot's choice leading him toward Sodom and into trouble, ch. 14; 19) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Lot lifted up his eyes, and beheld all the plain of Jordan, that it was well watered every where... even as the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt
Lot's worldly choice of the well-watered plain of Jordan
the choice by sight, for gain, heedless of Sodom's wickedness
the deceptive fairness masking the coming judgment, in contrast to Abram's faith

schools.summary: Lot chooses the plain and parts from Abram: 'then Lot chose him all the plain of Jordan; and Lot journeyed east: and they separated themselves the one from the other' — Lot taking for himself the whole fertile plain of Jordan (the choicest part, as it then seemed, he choosing the best for himself, leaving the rest to Abram, the selfish choice of the worldly man, grasping the apparent advantage); 'Lot journeyed east' (he moving eastward, toward the plain and the cities, toward Sodom, v.12, the direction of his journey taking him toward the wicked cities, the move that would bring him into peril, Lot departing toward the world he had chosen); 'they separated themselves the one from the other' (Abram and Lot parting, each going his way, the peaceful separation that ended the strife, vv.7-9, the parting of the godly uncle and the worldly nephew, Lot toward Sodom, Abram remaining in Canaan, v.12, the separation that set the course of each man's history) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Clarke, Gill.

themes: Then Lot chose him all the plain of Jordan; and Lot journeyed east: and they separated themselves the one from the other
Lot's choice of the plain and the separation from Abram
the worldly nephew taking the best and moving toward Sodom
the peaceful parting that set the course of each man's history

schools.summary: Abram in Canaan, Lot toward Sodom: 'Abram dwelled in the land of Canaan, and Lot dwelled in the cities of the plain, and pitched his tent toward Sodom' — Abram remaining in the land of Canaan (the land of promise), while Lot dwelt among the cities of the plain, pitching his tent toward Sodom (the contrast between the godly uncle, content in the promised land, and the worldly nephew, drawing near the wicked city); Abram dwelling in Canaan (the land God had promised to his seed, 12:7, the patriarch, having yielded the choice, remaining in the land of promise, content with God's portion, the man of faith abiding where God had placed him); 'pitched his tent toward Sodom' (Lot moving his dwelling toward Sodom, the wicked city, v.13, the gradual drawing near to evil, he soon to dwell in Sodom itself, 14:12; 19:1, the first step of Lot's declension — pitching toward Sodom, then dwelling in it, the danger of choosing nearness to the wicked for worldly gain, the worldly choice leading toward spiritual peril) — Gill.

themes: Abram dwelled in the land of Canaan, and Lot dwelled in the cities of the plain, and pitched his tent toward Sodom
Abram dwelling in Canaan and Lot pitching toward Sodom
the godly uncle content in the land of promise, the worldly nephew near the wicked city
the first step of Lot's declension toward Sodom (14:12; 19:1)

schools.summary: The wickedness of Sodom: 'but the men of Sodom were wicked and sinners before the LORD exceedingly' — the inhabitants of Sodom, near whom Lot pitched (v.12), exceedingly wicked (their sin great and open, 'before the LORD,' in his sight, and provoking him, the city ripe for the judgment that would come, ch. 19, the note setting the peril of Lot's choice in sharp relief); the men of Sodom 'wicked (toward men, in injustice and violence) and sinners (toward God, in impiety and vice)' (their sin both against God and against man, the gross and abominable wickedness of Sodom, the sins later named, ch. 19; Ezekiel 16:49-50, the exceeding, ripe wickedness of the city); 'before the LORD exceedingly' (their sin open before God and provoking his wrath, the wickedness so great as to cry to heaven, 18:20, the note warning of Lot's danger in choosing to dwell near such a people, for worldly gain exposing himself and his family to the contagion and the judgment of Sodom, ch. 19, the peril of the worldly choice) — Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: But the men of Sodom were wicked and sinners before the LORD exceedingly
the exceeding wickedness of the men of Sodom
their sin against God and man, the city ripe for judgment (Ezekiel 16:49-50)
the peril of Lot's choice to dwell near such a people

schools.summary: The LORD renews the promise after Lot's departure: 'and the LORD said unto Abram, after that Lot was separated from him, Lift up now thine eyes, and look from the place where thou art northward, and southward, and eastward, and westward' — God renewing his promise to Abram immediately after Lot had parted from him (the timing marked — after Abram's generous self-denial, vv.8-9, God coming to comfort and reward him, the patriarch, who had yielded the choice to Lot, now receiving the assurance of God's gift of the whole land); God bidding Abram 'lift up now thine eyes, and look... northward, and southward, and eastward, and westward' (to survey the whole land in every direction, in contrast to Lot, who 'lifted up his eyes,' v.10, for his own gain, Abram bidden by God to lift up his eyes and behold the land God would give him, the whole land, in all directions, promised to Abram and his seed); God's renewing the promise after Abram's self-denial showing that the man who yields his rights for peace loses nothing (God giving Abram the whole land, far more than the plain Lot chose, the reward of faith and charity, the comfort of God to his servant, the contrast between Lot's grasping choice and Abram's God-given inheritance) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And the LORD said unto Abram, after that Lot was separated from him, Lift up now thine eyes, and look... northward, and southward, and eastward, and westward
the LORD renewing the promise of the land to Abram after Lot's departure
the reward of Abram's self-denial and charity, given the whole land
Abram bidden to lift up his eyes by God, in contrast to Lot's choice by sight (v.10)

schools.summary: The land promised for ever: 'for all the land which thou seest, to thee will I give it, and to thy seed for ever' — God promising Abram the whole land he beheld (the land of Canaan, in all directions, v.14, the grant of the land to Abram, though he himself possessed none of it, Acts 7:5, but received it in promise, and to his seed, the covenant gift of the land confirmed and enlarged); 'to thy seed for ever' (the land promised to Abram's seed, his posterity, Israel, for ever, the duration 'for ever' variously understood: some taking it of the literal, perpetual possession of Canaan by Abram's natural seed, Israel, to the end of the present age, the land given to that part of his seed which descended through Isaac and Jacob, possessed until the consummation; others reading 'for ever' as a long but not strictly endless duration, the Hebrew olam meaning a long age, the period appointed; a spiritual/typological reading adding that the land-promise points beyond the earthly Canaan to the heavenly inheritance, the eternal rest of the seed, Hebrews 11:10, 16, Abraham looking for the city with foundations, the earthly land a type of the everlasting inheritance); the perpetual grant resting on God's covenant faithfulness (whether read of the literal land to Israel for the appointed age, or as pointing to the heavenly inheritance, the promise sure, the land given to Abram's seed by God's unbreakable word, the covenant of grace embracing both the earthly type and the heavenly substance) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

debates.question: In what sense is the land given to Abram's seed 'for ever'?

sides.position: The literal, perpetual possession of Canaan by Abram's natural seed (Israel) for the appointed age, until the consummation
A long but bounded duration (olam = a long age), the appointed period
A typological pointing beyond earthly Canaan to the heavenly inheritance, the eternal rest of the seed (Hebrews 11:10, 16)

themes: For all the land which thou seest, to thee will I give it, and to thy seed for ever
the promise of the land to Abram's seed for ever (the crux of the land-grant)
literal perpetual Canaan vs a bounded 'for ever' (olam) vs the heavenly inheritance (Hebrews 11:10, 16)
the grant sure by God's covenant faithfulness

schools.summary: The seed as the dust of the earth: 'and I will make thy seed as the dust of the earth: so that if a man can number the dust of the earth, then shall thy seed also be numbered' — God promising Abram a posterity as numberless as the dust of the earth (the promise of a vast multitude of descendants, to a man whose wife was barren, 11:30, God promising an innumerable seed, the human impossibility against which the promise shines); the hyperbole of multitude — 'if a man can number the dust of the earth, then shall thy seed also be numbered' (as the dust cannot be counted, so Abram's seed beyond numbering, the vast multitude of his descendants, Israel, and the multitude of his spiritual seed, all believers, Romans 4:16-18, Galatians 3:7, 29, the immense fulfillment of the promise); the promise of an innumerable seed to a childless old man calling for faith (Abram believing God, who quickeneth the dead and calleth things that are not as though they were, Romans 4:17-21, the promise embracing both his natural seed, Israel, and his spiritual seed, all who are of faith, of every nation, the numberless multitude of the faithful, the children of Abraham) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And I will make thy seed as the dust of the earth: so that if a man can number the dust of the earth, then shall thy seed also be numbered
the promise of a seed as numberless as the dust of the earth
the vast multitude of Abram's descendants, natural and spiritual (Romans 4:16-18)
the promise to a barren man, calling for the faith that believes God who quickens the dead

schools.summary: Abram bidden to walk the land: 'arise, walk through the land in the length of it and in the breadth of it; for I will give it unto thee' — God bidding Abram arise and walk through the land, in its length and breadth (to traverse and survey the land that God would give him, the patriarch invited to take possession, as it were, by walking through it, the symbolic taking of the land in faith); Abram to walk through the land, length and breadth (to view and traverse the whole, the act of walking through it a kind of symbolic possession, a customary token of ownership, Abram bidden to make the land his own, in faith, by traversing it, the patriarch taking possession of the promise by walking the land); 'for I will give it unto thee' (the ground of the command God's gift, God to give the land to Abram, and his seed, v.15, the patriarch walking through it not as owning it yet, Acts 7:5, but in faith of God's promise, the assurance of the gift confirmed by the invitation to walk through it) — Gill, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: Arise, walk through the land in the length of it and in the breadth of it; for I will give it unto thee
God bidding Abram walk through the land he would give him
the symbolic taking of possession by walking the land, in faith
the patriarch walking it not as owning it yet (Acts 7:5) but in faith of the promise

schools.summary: Abram dwells at Mamre and builds an altar: 'then Abram removed his tent, and came and dwelt in the plain of Mamre, which is in Hebron, and built there an altar unto the LORD' — Abram moving his tent and settling at Mamre, near Hebron (a place in the south of Canaan, the patriarch, still a tent-dweller, Hebrews 11:9, making Mamre his chief dwelling-place, Hebron, a place of importance in Abram's history, and his burial-place, ch. 23); Abram dwelling at Mamre (named from an Amorite ally of Abram, 14:13, the patriarch settling in the oaks of Mamre, his principal residence in Canaan, the place from which much of his later history unfolds, chs. 14; 18, the dwelling of the man of faith in the land of promise); 'built there an altar unto the LORD' (wherever Abram dwelt, he building an altar and worshiping God, as at Shechem, 12:7, and Bethel, 12:8, the patriarch's habit of worship, the altar at Mamre marking his devotion and his profession of the true God in the land, the man of faith setting up the worship of God in his dwelling-place) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: Then Abram removed his tent, and came and dwelt in the plain of Mamre, which is in Hebron, and built there an altar unto the LORD
Abram's removal to Mamre in Hebron and his building of an altar
the tent-dwelling man of faith making Mamre his chief dwelling (Hebrews 11:9)
the patriarch's habit of worship, setting up the altar wherever he dwelt
